S1: Correspondence: Vibeke Siewers, Department of Neurology, Oslo University Hospital, Ullevaal, PO Box 4956 Nydalen, 0424 Oslo, Norway.
S3: Siewers@ous-hf.no (Received 24 May 2013 ; accepted 7 August 2013 ) for patients, carers and assistants, and may have an impact on how patients perceive their condition (6 – 8).
S4: The introduction and process of incorpora- tion of new devices can initially be a disruptive experience, followed by the renegotiation of a new personal and social equilibrium and redrawing or modiﬁ cation of existing coping mechanisms, strategies and styles of adjustment (8).
S5: To our knowledge there are no studies exploring experiences with using an MI-E device.
S6: Sociological perspectives that conceptualize technological entities as “ actors ” in social networks may foster an understanding of the processes that are involved when an MI-E device is introduced in the home environment of patients with ALS (9).
S7: In this setting, the network constitutes the patient, the MI-E device, informal carers, health workers in the community health service and the hospital (10).
S8: ORIGINAL ARTICLE Experiences with using mechanical in – exsufﬂ ation in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis VIBEKE SIEWERS 1 , TRYGVE HOLM Ø Y 2,3 & JAN C. FRICH 1,4 1 Department of Neurology, Oslo University Hospital, Oslo, Norway, 2 Department of Neurology, Akershus University Hospital, L ø renskog, Norway, 3 Division of Clinical Medicine, University of Oslo, Oslo, Norway, 4 Institute of Health and Society, University of Oslo, Oslo, Norway Abstract Objectives: To explore patients ’ , family carers ’ and health professionals ’ experiences with using mechanical insufﬂ ation – exsufﬂ ation (MI-E) in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) in the home setting.
S9: Methods: Qualitative study using data from semi-structured interviews with ﬁ ve patients with ALS, three family carers and three health professionals.
S10: Results: Factors associated with successful implementation of the MI-E device were trust between patient and carer, conﬁ dence in using the device, and sufﬁ cient instructions and practical training.
S11: Introducing an MI-E device could change social relationships between patient and carers.
S12: One patient reported not using MI-E, others used the device on a regular basis, and some patients used it sporadically depending on symptoms or the presence of carers who were conﬁ dent with the device.
S13: Among effects reported by patients were elimination of airway secretions, feeling of being able to breathe more deeply and security in knowing that they had a device that could relieve symptoms in case they needed it.
S14: Conclusions: Health professionals need to take into account individual and social aspects of implementing an MI-E device in patients ’ home environment.
S15: Proper instruction and training is important for building competency in how to use the device, and trust and continuity among carers are important factors for successful implementation.
S16: Key words: Carers , cough , home care services , insufﬂ ation Introduction Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is a progressive neurodegenerative disease that causes respiratory complications.
S17: Patients with respiratory failure and impaired cough can experience difﬁ culties with elimination of airway secretion.
S18: Respiratory sup- port prolongs life and increases quality of life in patients with ALS (1 – 3), and mechanical insufﬂ ation – exsufﬂ ation (MI-E) can be used in elimination of secretions for patients with impaired cough (4).
S19: MI-E is a method to assist coughing, and it involves a deep insufﬂ ation by a pressure blower followed immediately by forced exsufﬂ a- tion.
S20: The device can be used with a mask covering the nose and mouth, except by patients with severe bulbar dysfunction (5).
S21: MI-E can also be used on a tracheotomy.
S22: Previous research suggests that medical technology may give rise to new challenges European Journal of Physiotherapy, 2013; 15: 201–207 ISSN 2167-9169 print/ISSN 2167-9177 online © 2013 Informa Healthcare DOI: 10.3109/21679169.2013.834513 European Journal of Physiotherapy Downloaded from informahealthcare.com by Oslo universitetssykehus Aker on 06/02/14 For personal use only.
S24: Interviews Eleven qualitative semi-structured in-depth inter- views were conducted in the participants ’ homes, current residence or working facilities.
S25: The inter- views lasted between 20 and 55 min and were carried out by the ﬁ rst author (VS), under the supervision of an experienced qualitative researcher (JCF).
S26: The interviewer had a background as a physiothera- pist in the ALS team, but was not active in the team when the study was conducted.
S27: The interviews were informed by an interview guide that was pre- pared based on existing literature and the research teams ’ experiences from the practice ﬁ eld.
S28: The interviews centred on exploring user conﬁ dence, and how using the MI-E device inﬂ uenced relationships and the patients ’ illness perception.
S29: The interviews were loosely structured trying to keep an open dia- logue and the interview guide was used only to secure that the speciﬁ c topics were covered.
S30: One patient had a tracheotomy at the time of the inter- view and wrote down the answers.
S31: The other patient with speech difﬁ culties relied on assistance from a family member.
S32: The interviews were digitally recorded and subsequently transcribed verbatim .
S33: Analyses Data from the interviews were analysed by all authors using a procedure for thematic content analysis, as described by Malterud (13).
S34: The analysis included four steps: (i) reading the material to obtain an over- all impression and bracketing previous preconcep- tions; (ii) identifying units of meaning representing different aspects of using MI-E, and coding for these; (iii) condensing and summarizing contents of each coded group; and (iv) making generalized descrip- tions and concepts regarding the implementation of the MI-E. Illustrating quotes from the interviews were translated from Norwegian to English by the ﬁ rst author in the process of writing the article.
S35: Results The patients comprised four men and one woman, age 43 – 81 years, with an ALS Functional Rating Scale-revised (ALSFRS-R (14)) score of 6 – 25 (median 13).
S36: Patients ’ functional level and ALSFRS-R scores are presented in Table I.
S37: The carers included three professional community nurses and three family members, a total of ﬁ ve women and one man.
S38: We identiﬁ ed several themes related to patients and their carers ’ experiences with using the MI-E device: trust and conﬁ dence, learning to use the device, individualized use of MI-E and perceived effects.
S39: Knowledge about patients ’ and their carers ’ per- spectives on management of ALS may help health professionals to accommodate to patients ’ needs and preferences (11).
S40: In this study, we tried to under- stand more about factors that could promote an appropriate implementation and use of MI-E. How do patients actually use the MI-E device?
S41: Is there sufﬁ cient transference of skills and competencies between different health professionals and carers?
S42: The aim of this study was to explore patients ’ , family carers ’ and health professionals ’ experiences of using an MI-E device in ALS in the home setting.
S43: Methods Setting Oslo University Hospital, Ullevaal, has a multidisci- plinary ALS team.
S44: The team consists of a neuro- logist, a nurse, an occupational therapist, a physiotherapist, a social worker and a nutritionist, and has established collaboration with pulmonolo- gists and gastroenterologists.
S45: The MI-E device is offered to patients, following standard criteria of use (5,12).
S46: The patients and the persons helping in managing the MI-E device were instructed and trained in using the equipment, either during the hospital stay or in the outpatient clinic.
S47: A physio- therapist from the hospital conducted follow up sessions to ensure correct use of the equipment.
S48: Sample A total of 11 informants, ﬁ ve patients with ALS who were using MI-E with a mask (Cough Assist ® Emerson), three healthcare professionals and three family carers were recruited through the multidisci- plinary ALS team at Oslo University Hospital from 2009 to 2010.
S49: Six patients were invited to par- ticipate, of whom one declined.
S50: We included two patients with speech difﬁ culties, but we experienced challenges conducting the interviews, and did there- fore not invite other patients with severe speech dif- ﬁ culties.
S51: We deliberately recruited carers with a variety in background and experiences in using the MI-E device.
S53: Approval was obtained from the Regional Committee for Medical Research Ethics, Health Region South-East.
S54: All participants were informed verbally and in writing prior to the study and gave their written consent.
S55: Conﬁ dentiality was main- tained using restricted, secure access to the data and anonymization of transcripts.
S56: European Journal of Physiotherapy Downloaded from informahealthcare.com by Oslo universitetssykehus Aker on 06/02/14 For personal use only.
S57: Mechanical in – exsufﬂ ation in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis 203 Trust and conﬁ dence Conﬁ dence with the technology and a trustful rela- tionship between patient and carer were reported as important preconditions for successful use of the MI-E device.
S58: In particular, patients had to have con- ﬁ dence in the skills of the person assisting them in using the device.
S59: The family members described the use of the MI-E as a new meeting point where they had been given a new tool to inﬂ uence the situation.
S60: Using the MI-E device could strengthen the relation- ship with the patient, as expressed by a female carer: Yes, that I can help him with something [ … ] it is … what is it exactly?
S61: But he wants me to do it … so it is, what can I say … a possibility to take care of him.
S62: Professional carers experienced that their conﬁ dence in using the technology was important for successful use of the device, and that lack of skills could take the focus away from the patient and cause anxiety.
S63: Health professionals reported that using the MI-E made the patient even more dependent on them, and only a limited number of nurses would be conﬁ dent with the technology and have the patient ’ s trust.
S64: A male nurse noted that different carers could have different experiences with using the MI-E in the same patient: No I think it has to do with trust.
S65: Because [ he ] is more insecure when others are coming [ … ] not those who use it, everyone can learn to use the machine, but he doesn ’ t feel conﬁ dent when there are new people coming.
S66: The use of MI-E called for continuity in the nursing staff, but in practice, this could be difﬁ cult to achieve due to work shifts, vacations and sick leave among the personnel.
S67: Lack of continuity could cause uncer- tainty and concerns that inﬂ uenced both patients and nursing staff in negative ways.
S68: Patients and professional carers underlined that good communi- cation between the patient and the person helping was important for successful use of the device.
S69: Learning to use the device Patients, family carers and health professionals reported that sufﬁ cient instruction and practical training in managing the MI-E device were necessary for successful use.
S70: Most patients and family carers said that they had been sufﬁ ciently trained, but lack of instruction and practical training was reported as a problem in settings where several health profession- als and informal carers were involved in the care of the patient.
S71: This could be the case regardless of whether the training had been performed while the patient was hospitalized or at the outpatient clinic.
S72: Patients, health professionals and family carers emphasized that the training should give a thorough understanding of the use of the MI-E, extending the plain instructions on how to handle the machine, so that those who were involved had the qualiﬁ cations to do adjustments if necessary.
S73: A male patient said: There can be situations where you have to do some improvisation and you need a deeper understanding beyond that this button shall be set on two.
S74: And it ’ s not very complicated either.
S75: I think that will be important.
S76: Some patients reported that the implementation of a technological device made the illness progression more apparent to them, as the device became a sign of declining respiratory function.
S77: A male patient commented: Well it helps me with things I can ’ t do any more.
S78: At the same time that means I ’ ve become worse.
S79: It is in a way not the machines that are better, but there are new machines because a new func- tion is gone.
S80: That ’ s how it feels mentally.
S82: Description of the patients ’ Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis Functional Rating Scale-revised (ALSFRS-R) scores (0-48), months of using mechanical insufﬂ ation – exsufﬂ ation (MI-E), functional level and where the training took place.

S84: European Journal of Physiotherapy Downloaded from informahealthcare.com by Oslo universitetssykehus Aker on 06/02/14 For personal use only.
S86: Some patients needed time to accept that the illness progressed and were at ﬁ rst reluctant to use the device.
S87: Such processes could complicate the transference of skills to carers, as recounted by a male patient: In the beginning it was just placed there (smiles).
S88: I had all different kinds of excuses why I wouldn ’ t use it.
S89: But now I ’ ve realized that it helps me.
S90: Both family and professional carers expressed differ- ent needs concerning the practical training.
S91: While some wanted to have learning sessions in groups, others preferred individual supervision from one competent person.
S92: Some professional carers explained that good intentions in attending a training program from the hospital could be jeopardized by lack of time and other priorities.
S93: Individualized use of MI-E Patients used the MI-E device differently.
S94: Some patients reported regular use every day regardless of effect, as explained by a male patient: I use it every morning, in the middle of the day, and in the evening.
S95: It ’ s not always I cough up mucus, but in the mornings it ’ s normally – bingo – effective.
S96: Other patients used the device only when they expe- rienced problems with secretions.
S97: The complexity of the disease, with patients experiencing different physical or psychological symptoms, affected how and when to use the MI-E, as emphasized by a male patient: It is difﬁ cult to establish routines, because I never know, as I said, due to gas in the stomach, if I can do things.
S98: For example, it would have been good to use it three times a day, or when assistants shift, but this is not how it will be.
S99: Some patients used the MI-E irregularly because the carer at hand lacked competence in using the device.
S100: Perceived effects of MI-E Most patients experienced effects of using the MI-E on elimination of airway secretions, and some felt they were able to “ breathe more deeply ” .
S101: One patient with bulbar symptoms experienced no such effect, and felt uncomfortable when using the device.
S102: Other patients experienced a comfort in knowing that they had a device to help them with elimination of airway secretions in case a need would occur, as con- veyed by a male patient: Yes it is that if I was to get something in my throat, I know it ’ s just the machine that can ﬁ x it and not the BiPAP [breathing machine], then I had to have it.
S103: So knowing that it is there, if it should happen, then we can just use it.
S104: Because the panic of being strangled, it ’ s something that strikes you many times each day.
S105: I have experienced that feeling and I do not forget it.
S106: For some patients, knowing that they had the machine within reach made them at ease so they could move more freely out of the house, even though they did not use the MI-E regularly.
S107: Discussion The aim of this study was to explore ALS patients ’ , their family carers ’ and health professionals ’ experi- ences with using an MI-E device in the home set- ting.
S108: We found that factors associated with successful implementation were trust between patient and the person helping, conﬁ dence in using the device and transference of skills.
S109: Introducing an MI-E device can change social relationships between patients and carers, and continuity of care becomes even more important.
S110: One patient reported not using MI-E, others used the device on a regular basis, and some patients used it sporadically depending on symp- toms or the presence of competent carers.
S111: The effects reported by patients varied from none to efﬁ cient elimination of airway secretions, feeling of being able to breathe more deeply and a feeling of safety in knowing that they had a device that could help if needed.
S112: Implications of MI-E on social networks A sociological perspective can display the diversity in the interaction between humans and technology, and thereby enlighten important aspects beyond the medical intent of the technical device.
S113: For patients using MI-E, a social network is constituted by the patient, the MI-E device, family carers and health workers in the community or in the hospital.
S114: A suc- cessful coordination of this network is an essential factor for obtaining optimal treatment (10).
S115: Our results underline that trust and conﬁ dence are important factors for patients and carers when implementing and using MI-E.
S116: Previous research suggests that using medical technology increases the need of a trustful relationship between the per- sons involved, and creates new dependencies and relationships (8,15 – 18).
S118: found that users ’ acceptance of technology was closely linked to user competency, and that user competency was European Journal of Physiotherapy Downloaded from informahealthcare.com by Oslo universitetssykehus Aker on 06/02/14 For personal use only.
S119: Mechanical in – exsufﬂ ation in amyotrophic lateral sclerosis 205 inﬂ uenced by the relationship between the patient and the carer (15,16).
S120: When applying MI-E, the carer needs to coordinate the use of the device with the patient ’ s breathing cycle, and thus both carer and patient needs to be conﬁ dent and able to com- municate.
S121: The severe prognosis of ALS, and the potential effects of MI-E, demonstrates that imple- mentation of technology in medical settings affects fundamental aspects of life such as health and risk of death, knowledge and uncertainty, autonomy and mobility, wonder, sorrow, hope and anxiety (7).
S122: Family members experienced that using the MI-E device together with the patient made their rela- tionship stronger.
S123: Health professionals expressed that using the MI-E device made patients more dependent on them, suggesting that continuity among the nursing staff is an important factor for successful implementation and use of the MI-E device.
S124: Building competence Our ﬁ ndings are in accordance with previous research showing that several factors may compli- cate patients ’ use of medical technology, including misunderstanding of use, questions that are raised without being sufﬁ ciently answered by the carer at hand, and lack of conﬁ dence and acceptance of using the device (15,16,19,20).
S125: When implement- ing the MI-E device in the home setting for patients with ALS, not only do the patient and family mem- bers have to be trained, but also other informal and professional carers.
S126: Our study indicates that patients experienced personnel who lacked skills and conﬁ dence in using the MI-E device.
S127: The health professionals who decide to implement the MI-E device need to have a realistic plan for the transference of skills to all persons involved.
S128: Such training should give an in-depth understand- ing of the purpose of the machine, as there will be need for adjustments as the disease progresses.
S129: Health professionals also need to transfer knowl- edge and evaluation tools in addition to skills.
S130: In the learning process, these assets often stay behind with the professionals (21).
S131: Patients in our study expressed that the imple- mentation of a technical device made the progression of the disease more apparent, and that this could make them reluctant to try new technology.
S132: Health professionals need to accommodate and recognize the continuous process of acceptance that patients go through when new technologies are introduced (18).
S133: Health professionals should also acknowledge that learning takes time, and that a successful imple- mentation of MI-E relies on building competency among patients and their carers.
S134: Individualized use of MI-E Our results suggest that individual patients could use the MI-E device regularly, sporadically or not at all.
S135: Patients ’ use of technology may differ from what health professionals have prescribed (17).
S136: Our study suggests that not only differences in secretion, but also other practical, physical or psychological factors inﬂ uenced how patients used the MI-E device.
S137: When technology is introduced in the home setting, the patient, family carers and health professionals may encounter new challenges that are not as easily antic- ipated by hospital staff.
S138: Corbin & Strauss (22) argue that patients man- age their daily life in the interplay between three different types of work; illness work (concerned with symptom management), every-day life work (con- cerned with practical tasks in daily life) and bio- graphical work (concerned with the ill person ’ s life history, goals, motivations and resources).
S139: Patients will constantly make priorities in their daily life between and within these types of work when man- aging an illness.
S140: For example, during days with aggravations of secretion symptoms, treatment with the MI-E device may be given priority.
S141: When these symptoms are not present, other physical or psycho- logical factors, like practical tasks, bad sleep or stomach ache, call for attention.
S142: A patient may thus make individual adjustments even if a health profes- sional has prescribed regular use of a device.
S143: Some patients in our study expressed anxiety of choking to death, and they experienced that they could trust the MI-E device to relieve their symp- toms.
S144: The device was a comfort to family carers and health professionals, knowing that they could cope if the patient had serious symptoms caused by stag- nation of airway secretions.
S145: Our ﬁ ndings suggest that patients also experienced an effect of using the MI-E device beyond evacuation of secretion.
S146: Some patients reported that just knowing they had the device enabled them to carry out activities outside their homes, because they trusted that the MI-E device would be available if needed.
S147: These ﬁ ndings indicate that the device inﬂ uenced patients ’ capacity to manage daily life.
S148: Use of technology in ALS care Our study addresses the implications of using technology in managing a severe disease.
S149: Medical technology, including a variety of ventilators, com- municators and environmental control devices, is increasingly used to prolong life and relieve symp- toms for patients with ALS, and may in the future also comprise brain computer interfaces (1,2).
S150: Although an important aim is to help patients to maintain their autonomy, our results suggest that European Journal of Physiotherapy Downloaded from informahealthcare.com by Oslo universitetssykehus Aker on 06/02/14 For personal use only.
S152: implementing new technology may also have other effects on the relationship between patients and carers.
S153: If the patient is not able to use the technol- ogy without help, he or she may actually become more dependent on the carer ’ s skills (23).
S154: On the other hand, conﬁ dent and competent use of such technology may actually enable patients to main- tain autonomy.
S155: Notably, the care for people with ALS in their homes is usually performed by people without medical education or training in the use of medical technology.
S156: Our results on the implemen- tation of MI-E is likely transferrable to other med- ical technology, and underscore the need for education, training and acceptance of individual responses and preferences.
S157: Further research is needed to understand fully the use and effect of technology in ALS care.
S158: Methodological considerations This study has a small number of informants with a majority of male patients and was performed in a restricted period.
S159: Data from the interviews nev- ertheless display varieties in experiences, and our ﬁ ndings suggest factors that could be used to pro- mote implementation and use of MI-E. Informants were recruited from one clinic, and their experi- ences thus partly depend upon the routines for training patients and carers in this clinic.
S160: The fact that the interviewer was known to some of the par- ticipants may limit the quality of the answers, but could also have promoted an open dialogue.
S161: We think that the ﬁ ndings in the present study expand our knowledge of patients and their carers ’ per- spectives on using an MI-E device and that they can have relevance for other ALS clinics.
S162: Conclusions Health professionals need to take into account indi- vidual and social aspects of implementing an MI-E device in patients ’ home environment.
S163: Proper instruction and practical training and conﬁ dence in how to use the device, and trust and continuity among carers are important factors for successful implementation.
S164: Acknowledgements We thank all our informants for their contribution.
S165: Conﬂ icts of interest: None to declare.
